name: Manzey, Dietrich
recent_1: Rieger, T., Marx, B., & Manzey, D. (2025). Likelihood systems can improve hit rates in low-prevalence visual search over binary systems. Human Factors, 67(2), 152-167. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251320589
recent_1_synopsis: This study compared likelihood (graded-confidence) alarm systems against conventional binary alarms in a low-prevalence visual search task, where rare targets typically erode detection performance. Likelihood systems improved hit rates relative to binary systems, suggesting that conveying graded diagnostic information helps operators calibrate attention and catch rare critical events.
recent_2: Rieger, T., Onnasch, L., Roesler, E., & Manzey, D. (2025). Why highly reliable decision support systems often lead to suboptimal performance and what we can do about it. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(5), 736-745. DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2025.3584662
recent_2_synopsis: The paper explains the counterintuitive finding that joint human-automation performance with highly reliable decision aids frequently falls short of the aid's standalone reliability, tracing this to complacency, automation bias, and over-reliance. It synthesizes the mechanisms and proposes design and procedural interventions to recover the performance potential of accurate decision support.
recent_3: Rieger, T., Kugler, L., Manzey, D., & Roesler, E. (2024). The (im)perfect automation schema: Who is trusted more, automated or human decision support? Human Factors, 66(8), 1995-2007. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231197347
recent_3_synopsis: Using a decision-support paradigm, this study tested whether people hold an "imperfect automation schema" that leads them to trust automated advisors differently from human advisors of equal reliability. The results clarify how the perceived agent type (automated vs. human) shapes trust and reliance, informing how decision aids should be framed to operators.
seminal_1: Parasuraman, R., & Manzey, D. H. (2010). Complacency and bias in human use of automation: An attentional integration. Human Factors, 52(3), 381-410. DOI: 10.1177/0018720810376055
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark review integrates the literature on automation complacency and automation bias, arguing that both arise from common attention-allocation mechanisms under multitask load and occur in novices and experts alike. With roughly 3,000+ citations, it is among the most influential theoretical accounts of automation-induced performance failures and frames much subsequent work on trust, reliance, and decision support.
seminal_2: Manzey, D., Reichenbach, J., & Onnasch, L. (2012). Human performance consequences of automated decision aids: The impact of degree of automation and system experience. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 6(1), 57-87. DOI: 10.1177/1555343411433844
seminal_2_synopsis: This experimental paper dissects how the degree of automation and accumulated system experience jointly shape operators' commission and omission errors when using imperfect decision aids. Cited roughly 500+ times, it provides foundational empirical evidence that higher automation can amplify automation bias and that experience does not reliably immunize users against it.
expertise_overview: Studies human-automation interaction, with a central focus on how people trust, rely on, and are degraded by automated and AI-based decision support. Core themes include automation complacency and automation bias, the calibration of trust and reliance, and the consequences of varying degrees and stages of automation for human performance and error. Investigates alarm and alert design, contrasting binary alarms with likelihood (graded) systems in visual search and monitoring, particularly under time pressure and low target prevalence. Also examines human-robot interaction, including how anthropomorphism and failure type affect trust attitudes versus trust behavior. Methods are primarily controlled laboratory experiments with performance, error, and subjective-trust measures; application domains span aviation, medical/diagnostic monitoring, and human performance in extreme environments such as space and Antarctica.
notes: Identified with high confidence via Google Scholar (user=EmFhGH8AAAAJ), ResearchGate, dblp, and TU Berlin association; the hf_papers anchor DOIs (all listing Manzey as author) match exactly. Recent papers prioritize 2024-2025 venues. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar as of mid-2026: the 2010 Parasuraman & Manzey review is one of his most-cited works (~3,000+); the 2012 JCEDM paper ~500+. All four recent and both seminal DOIs were verified via Crossref API (200 + matching title/authors). No unverifiable citations.
